## Item 2A: Naloxone (Narcan) Administration Protocol

**Presenter:** Barbara Robertson, Director of Health Services

### Key Points:
- **Fentanyl crisis:** Illicitly manufactured fentanyl is now the leading cause of death for ages 18-45 in the U.S. (per DEA/CDC/Governor's office data). Any drug not prescribed and purchased from a legitimate pharmacy carries risk, as illicit fentanyl is being mixed into many substances including cocaine and THC vape products.
- **How naloxone works:** Displaces opioids from receptors controlling breathing, sedation, and blood pressure. Has no effect on someone without opioids in their system.
- **Legal basis:** Health and Safety Code 483.102–483.106, policies FFAC.
- **Deployment:** Narcan (nasal spray only) is now stocked at:
  - Junior high and high school clinics
  - High school athletic training rooms
  - All Conroe ISD police officers
  - Moorehead Stadium and WoodForest Bank Stadium
- **Training completed:** All junior high and high school nurses and all CISD police officers trained on signs/symptoms of opioid overdose, administration of naloxone, and supporting the individual until EMS arrives.
- **Program launched January 4, 2023** when students returned to secondary campuses.
- **Shelf life:** FDA now manufacturing with a 3-year expiration.
- **Dosing:** Each box contains 2 doses; multiple boxes stocked per location because overdoses often come in pairs (students sharing substances). Texas A&M training indicated both doses may be needed per person.
- **Summer training:** District participated in a mock mass overdose exercise at Caney Creek in conjunction with Montgomery County — this experience helped drive urgency for the program.
- **Special thanks** to Dr. Joy Lanto and the Texas A&M Opioid Task Force for ~10 months of collaboration to launch the program.

---

## Item 2B: MEOP Audit Submission to Texas School Safety Center

**Presenter:** Ethan Martin, School Safety Coordinator

### Key Points:
- The Texas School Safety Center required submission of the district's Multi-Hazard Emergency Operation Plan (MEOP), basic plan, active threat annex, and active shooter appendix.
- Submitted approximately one month before the deadline.
- **Initial result:** District received a "deficient" notice (18 pages of deficiencies). Upon review, Martin found that many items flagged as deficient were actually included in the plan but not formatted to the state's preferred framework.
- After reformatting to align with the state's template while maintaining CISD's existing approved plan, the submission was **approved with no additional information required**.
- Total documentation submitted included campus-based MEOPs totaling approximately **16,000 pages**.
- **Notable:** Dr. Null reported that no district's plan was accepted on first submission. Multiple superintendents from other districts reached out for help; Ethan Martin personally assisted **three other districts** in getting their plans approved.
- The Texas School Safety Center recently sent out a pre-submission checklist; Martin volunteered to provide feedback on the checklist. Next submission likely due **October 2024**.

---

## Item 2C: Review and Input for 2023-2024 Multi-Hazard Emergency Operations Plan

**Presenter:** Ethan Martin

### Key Points:
- Committee members will receive the current MEOP framework document (approximately 137–187 pages) via email for review and input.
- A deadline will be set for feedback submission.
- The plan will be presented for committee recommendation/approval at the **summer meeting**.
- Any proposed changes will be vetted through appropriate channels.
- Format is largely dictated by the state; limited flexibility on structure.
- **Reminder:** The document is internal/confidential — "your eyes only."

## Item 2D: Smart Tag System Implementation (Bus Safety)

**Presenters:** Chris McCord (Asst. Superintendent) and Sam (Director of Transportation)

### Overview:
Smart Tag is a tablet-and-badge system used on school buses for student identification, route management, and safety.

### Key Technical Details:
- **RFID badge cards** issued to all ~71,000 students (approximately 40,000 are bus riders)
- Cards contain only a **14-digit randomized ID number** — no battery, no GPS, no tracking capability
- Cards can only be read by CISD Smart Tag readers mounted on buses (must physically touch reader)
- System uses **cellular connectivity** (not Wi-Fi)
- Highest-level data security protocols followed

### Implementation Status:
- **Oak Ridge High School feeder:** Successfully implemented (several weeks)
- **Grand Oaks feeder:** Implemented starting January 2023; going well
- **The Woodlands feeder:** Planned for the following week but delayed ~1 week due to weather (badge printer company based in Austin unable to ship)
- **Goal:** Full implementation before spring break

### Safety Benefits Highlighted:
1. **Parent notifications:** Parents can opt in to receive text alerts when the bus approaches their stop — first time in 130 years parents will have this capability. Reduces time children wait exposed at bus stops.
2. **Confirming correct drop-offs:** Especially critical for PreK/K students and when substitute/standby drivers are used. System shows student photos and flags young students requiring authorized adult pickup.
3. **Substitute driver support:** Routes displayed on tablet; prevents wrong turns and getting lost (real-world example of standby driver ending up in Willis area shared).
4. **End-of-route child check:** System alerts drivers if students are still scanned as on-bus at route end — added layer beyond physical walk-through checks.
5. **Preventing unauthorized riders:** Helps confirm only CISD students board buses.
6. **Real-world success stories:**
   - January 24 flood: Principals in Oak Ridge feeder used system to confirm bus status for worried parents during street closures
   - First day at Suchma Elementary: Parent's concern about locating children resolved within minutes
   - Loading time improvement: Bradley Elementary loaded buses in 11 minutes vs. usual 15 minutes

### District Statistics:
- CISD buses drive **6.3 million miles/year** (14 times to moon and back)
- **~25 million** student on/off bus events per year
- Currently at **24 driver vacancies** (best staffing level in years, down from 30-34)

### Discussion Points:
- **Opt-out:** Available; only 1 parent has opted out across both implemented feeders so far
- **Lost badges:** Each school has its own badge printer for quick replacement; $5 replacement fee planned but no student will be denied a badge; students without badges are still transported (manual entry available)
- **Lanyards:** Breakaway lanyards provided for safety; personalization may be allowed in the future
- **Badge uses beyond bus:** Can be used at cafeteria point-of-sale and library checkout
- Every student receives a badge and lanyard regardless of rider status (students may become riders mid-year)

---

## Item 2E: Student ID Badges (Campus Identification)

**Presenters:** Chris McCord, with input from Dr. Null, Sandra Young, Libra Thompson, and others

### Current State:
- No CISD campuses currently require students to wear ID badges
- Conroe High School previously required them under a former principal (who is on the committee) but discontinued after principal's departure
- Grand Oaks began requiring ID badges as of the prior week (serving as informal pilot)

### Research Findings:
- Extensive research of major Texas districts and Montgomery County districts showed **CISD is an anomaly** — most comparable districts require student IDs
- Various models exist: all grades, 5-12, or 7-12
- District did not investigate enforcement practices at other districts

### Benefits Discussed:
- **Identifying who belongs on campus:** Critical for large secondary campuses (4,000-5,000+ students)
- **Preventing unauthorized individuals:** Real-world examples shared:
  - Three individuals from Westfield HS entered another campus intending to harm students — caught because they lacked IDs
  - A high school-age student from another district entered a CISD campus, passed three layers of adults, and was found in a restroom with a large quantity of drugs and $1,300+ cash
- **Reducing anonymity:** Teachers cannot know every student; IDs help identify students involved in incidents
- **Attendance potential:** Teachers expressed interest in using badges for automated attendance
- **Camera integration:** IDs help correlate surveillance footage with student identity

### Challenges Discussed:
- **Teenager pushback:** Students will test boundaries; task-avoidant students may exploit temporary ID processes to avoid class
- **Enforcement consistency:** Must be enforced uniformly across ALL staff and ALL campuses to be effective
- **Discipline escalation:** Repeated non-compliance leads to escalating consequences that can seem disproportionate
- **Counterfeiting concerns:** Acknowledged but mitigated by changing badge design annually (landscape vs. portrait, background colors)
- **Faculty buy-in:** Essential for success; if some teachers don't enforce, system fails

### Committee Feedback:
- **High school principals want ID badges** and expect them
- Grand Oaks principal proactively started implementation
- **Strong support** from committee for moving forward
- Suggestion to start at **secondary level** (most critical due to campus fluidity, driving access, larger populations)
- Multiple members advocated for **district-wide implementation** (elementary through high school) to build early habits
- Acknowledged this involves multiple departments: principals, technology, processes for temporary IDs, etc.
- Parent/teacher member (Libra Thompson) reported positive personal experience with Grand Oaks implementation
- Committee member who previously lived in another district shared positive elementary ID experience

### Outcome:
- **No formal vote taken** — Dr. Null confirmed the committee's feedback supports exploring and moving toward implementation
- District will begin working with principals and technology departments on processes
- Grand Oaks serving as pilot/test case
- **Decision to be continued** at future meetings; may eventually come to school board as part of dress code

---

## Item 2F: TAC Chapter 61 Proposed Facility Security Requirements (Fencing)

**Presenters:** Chris McCord, Marshall Schrader (Director of Maintenance), Easy Foster (Director of Planning & Construction)

### Context:
- Texas is proposing new facility security requirements under TAC Chapter 61
- State has **not yet rendered final opinion** on minimum fencing requirements for "secured areas"
- A **grant with a February 17 deadline** is available for fencing improvements
- District wants to order fencing that will meet likely future state requirements and beat the expected rush from other districts

### Current District Status:
- All portables have been fenced in (major accomplishment, completed proactively)
- Egress gates installed (significant investment)
- Current standard: 6-foot black vinyl-coated chain link fence
- Some campuses still have 4-foot fencing
- District already locks/secures all perimeter doors (exceeds likely state requirements)
- Impact-resistant window film currently applied at front entry vestibules only

### Proposed State Framework:
- **"Secured area"** concept: If an area is fenced to meet the state's secure definition, schools gain operational flexibility on door/window treatment within that area
- Without a secured area: All doors and glass near doors around the building must have impact-resistant film
- Likely acceptable fencing options:
  - **6-foot fence with privacy slats** (anti-scalable feature — prevents toe-holds)
  - **8-foot fence** (with or without slats)
  - Other options (rod iron, stone) exist but are significantly more expensive

### Cost Comparison (per linear foot):
- 8-foot chain link (no slats): ~$5 cheaper than 6-foot with slats
- 8-foot with slats: ~$20 more than 8-foot without slats

### Maintenance/Planning Considerations:
- **Maintenance preference:** 8-foot non-slatted (easier to maintain; slats degrade over time, require hurricane prep)
- **Aesthetic concerns:** 8-foot fencing can look institutional/prison-like, especially at elementary campuses
- **Wind concerns:** Slats add wind load; do not come out easily for storm prep
- **Windscreens** (like at Rice and Paw elementary playgrounds): Can serve as anti-scalable feature on 6-foot fence; allow seeing out but not in; can include school logos; ~$4,000 for significant length; ~5-year lifespan; require zip-tie replacement after storm removal

### Committee Discussion & Direction:
- **Consensus emerged** for a **flexible, campus-by-campus approach:**
  - **8-foot non-slatted fencing** in back/playground areas (not publicly visible, maximum security)
  - **6-foot slatted fencing** in forward-facing/visible areas (better aesthetics while meeting anti-scalable requirements)
  - Windscreens with logos on forward-facing areas where appropriate
  - Not a one-size-fits-all approach; administrator discretion by campus
- Some older campuses with 4-foot brick walls will require creative solutions
- Where secure areas cannot be created, district will comply by applying impact-resistant film to windows/doors
- **Urgency emphasized:** District wants to place orders ahead of statewide demand surge expected when rules are finalized

---

## Item 2G: Texas School Safety Center Intruder Detection Audits

**Presenter:** Chris McCord

### Audit Results:
- Governor Abbott mandated intruder detection audits starting January 2022
- **6 campuses audited in January 2023** → total of 19 campuses audited to date
- **10 campuses** scheduled for audit in February
- **2 campuses had findings** (one phase each) out of 19 total
- **CISD success rate: 89%** (17 of 19 passed) vs. **state average: 71%**

### Required Actions for Findings:
- Safety and Security Committee must meet (accomplished at this meeting)
- Develop a detailed training plan for the 2 campuses with findings
- **Training scheduled for February 17** (inservice day) for all staff, auxiliary, and subs at those campuses
- Follow-up meetings planned to catch anyone who misses the February 17 training
- Training plan must be submitted to Texas School Safety Center by **March 13** (will be submitted before spring break)

### Additional District Initiative:
- **All campuses** (not just those with findings) will view a new perimeter door safety and security video on February 17
- Video created by Andrew Stewart; premiered at the principal/director update meeting earlier that day
- All campuses will also review existing access control procedures
